question: Your fleet is at 24 instances doing 19,000 req/s. You double it to 48. Throughput falls to 17,400 req/s, p99 rises, CPU per instance drops from 61% to 34%, and nothing errors. What is the most likely explanation?
options: A bad deploy shipped alongside the scale-up; the new instances are running different code.
The load balancer has not finished redistributing connections, and throughput will recover once it does.
Throughput as a function of fleet size has a peak, and you have crossed it — the coordination cost between instances now grows faster than the capacity they add.
The database has become the bottleneck; you need read replicas before adding more application instances.
explanation: The falling per-instance CPU is the clue that rules out the other three. A bad deploy or a saturated database would show something pinned at 100% and something erroring; instead every instance is healthier than before and the system is slower. That combination — more machines, less throughput, lower per-node utilization, no errors — has one cause: you are past the peak of the scalability curve, in the retrograde region. A load-balancer rebalance settles in seconds, not minutes, and would not depress total throughput below its starting value.

question: A service has a serial fraction σ = 0.05. According to Amdahl's Law, what does a 100-machine fleet deliver, and what is the ceiling?
options: 100x, and the ceiling is set by hardware rather than by σ.
16.81x, and the ceiling is 1/σ = 20x no matter how many machines you buy.
95x, because only 5% of the work is lost to serialization.
50x, and the ceiling is 2/σ = 40x.
explanation: Work it longhand: the job costs σ + (1−σ)/N = 0.05 + 0.95/100 = 0.0595, so C = 1/0.0595 = 16.81. The tempting answer is 95x, because '5% serialized' sounds like '5% lost'. It is not a 5% tax — it is a hard floor on the time the job takes, so as N grows the parallel part vanishes and the total time bottoms out at σ itself. That gives a ceiling of 1/0.05 = 20x. Five percent serialization means 83 of your 100 machines produce nothing.

question: Why is the coherency term in the Universal Scalability Law written as κN(N−1) rather than κN?
options: It is an empirical curve-fitting choice; the quadratic simply fits measured data better than a linear term.
Because coordination cost is counted per PAIR of nodes, and N nodes have N(N−1)/2 pairs — the count of relationships grows as the square of the count of nodes.
Because each node must be checked twice, once when it joins the cluster and once when it leaves.
Because latency between any two nodes doubles each time the fleet doubles.
explanation: The quadratic is not a fitting convenience, it is a count. Four nodes have 6 pairs; eight nodes have 28 — doubling the nodes multiplied the pairs by 4.7. N(N−1) is the number of directed exchanges, once in each direction. That is why a cache-invalidation broadcast, a full service mesh, or N app instances each connected to M database replicas gets disproportionately expensive: you are not adding nodes, you are adding relationships between nodes, and relationships grow as N squared while capacity grows as N.

question: You sweep your service at N = 1, 2, 4, 8, 12 and 16 instances — every level you have ever run in production — and the USL fits all six points with under 2% error. What is wrong with using the resulting N* to plan capacity?
options: Nothing; a fit with under 2% error across six points is exactly what a trustworthy fit looks like.
Six points is too few for any two-parameter fit; you need at least twenty.

Every point is on the rising side of the curve, so many different (σ, κ) pairs fit them equally well while disagreeing wildly about where the peak is — the data contains no information about the knee.
The fit is fine, but you must convert throughput to latency before computing N*.
explanation: This is the failure mode that matters, and a good residual is exactly what makes it dangerous. The lesson's code demonstrates it: fitting only the points at N <= 16 produced sigma = 0.0163 and kappa = 0.001599, fitted all seven points, and then predicted 70.2 req/s at N = 64 where the truth was 83.7 — 16% wrong, in the direction that buys hardware. Six points is plenty if they straddle the knee. The requirement is not more points, it is points on both sides, which means deliberately load-testing fleet sizes you would never run.

question: Two services both stop gaining throughput as you scale out. Service A's shared database sits at 99% busy and total throughput is flat. Service B's shared database is 67% busy — less busy than it was at half the fleet size — and total throughput is falling. Which is which, and what do you do?
options: Both are σ-limited; shard the database in both cases and both curves will improve.

A is σ-limited (shard it, add replicas, take the shared step off the hot path); B is κ-limited (subset the coordination, cell the fleet, put a pooler in front) — and B can be improved immediately by REMOVING machines.
A is κ-limited and B is σ-limited; the busier resource is always the coordination cost.
Both need more instances; the flat and falling curves are just measurement noise at high concurrency.
explanation: The discriminator is what the shared resource's utilization does as you add machines. Under a dominant σ term it climbs toward 100% and pins there, throughput plateaus, and removing machines changes nothing. Under a dominant κ term the shared resource gets LESS busy while throughput falls, because the time is going into coordination spread thinly across every node rather than piling up at one visible bottleneck — measured in this lesson at 80.5% busy at the peak versus 67.4% at twice the fleet, with 17.8% less throughput. Only B has a fix available in one deploy: scale in, which recovered +21.7%.

question: Sharding a serial resource in two halved σ (0.0249 to 0.0098) and raised peak throughput 13.7% — but the peak stayed at N = 32. Subsetting the coordination graph moved N* from 29 to 52. Why the difference?
options: Sharding was implemented incorrectly; a correct shard always moves the peak proportionally to the number of shards.
N* = sqrt((1−σ)/κ) is dominated by κ: halving σ changes the numerator by about 1%, while cutting κ by 69% moves N* by a factor of roughly 1/sqrt(0.31). σ sets the ceiling; κ sets where the peak is.
Subsetting also reduces σ, which is what actually moved the peak.
The peak position depends only on fleet size and instance type, not on the fitted coefficients.
explanation: Put the numbers in the formula. With σ around 0.02, the numerator (1−σ) is about 0.98 whichever way you move σ — halving it shifts N* by well under one percent. κ is under a square root in the denominator, so cutting it by 69% multiplies N* by about 1.8, which is exactly the 29-to-52 move measured. This is the practical content of 'σ caps you, κ kills you': sharding buys real throughput at the fleet size you already run, but it leaves the cliff exactly where it was. Note also that subsetting raised the fitted σ from 0.0249 to 0.0343 — it converted a quadratic cost into a linear one rather than deleting it, which is a trade you want but should not mistake for a free lunch.
